text: Discovering catalysts that facilitate the solar-to-hydrogen conversion at water-splitting efficiency remains an open and essential challenge. A key catalyst that must be identified is a highly efficient catalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Here, a series of mixed nickel-iron oxyhydroxides catalysts Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH are synthesized. A 500-fold increase in catalytic activity is realized in comparison with pure nickel and iron oxides—affording one of the most active catalysts reported in alkaline solution for the OER reaction. The catalytic sites and edge structures of the highly active NiFeOOH are elucidated at the operating condition using operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy. It is found that Fe ions located in the octahedral site have shorter Fe–O distances and the Fe3+ ions at such sites are likely to be on the edge-sharing sites of [NiO₆] octahedra. Further supported by the density functional theory calculations, optimal adsorption energies of oxygen evolution reaction intermediates are found at iron active sites, thus reducing the catalyst overpotential. The nickel sites serve only as a structure to hold on the iron active sites and play a passive role in the catalytic process.
